But this answer presupposes a preferred factorization of the universe into system + environment. What determines this factorization? In a fully quantum universe with no external observer — just one big wavefunction — where does the factorization come from?

This is called the preferred factorization problem or the problem of the preferred basis for branches.

It remains open.

VIII. What Does This Mean for Quantum Computing?

You might wonder: if the foundations are this unsettled, how can we build quantum computers?

The answer: quantum computers depend on the formalism, not the interpretation.

The unitary evolution of the Schrödinger equation — universally agreed upon — is what makes quantum computation possible. Superposition, entanglement, interference — all are features of the unitary formalism.

Quantum error correction — my own field — is entirely within the formalism. We describe errors as quantum channels, encode logical qubits in stabilizer codes, perform syndrome measurements to detect errors.

None of this requires a preferred interpretation. Copenhagen, Many-Worlds, Bohmian mechanics — all agree on the predictions. All would endorse the same quantum error correction protocols.

The measurement problem matters for:

  • Understanding what quantum computers are
  • Understanding what “running a quantum algorithm” means
  • Understanding whether quantum computers are doing something genuinely different from classical computers

For Everettians: each quantum computation branches the universe into a superposition of outcomes. Quantum advantage comes from interference between branches.

For Bohmians: the wavefunction guides physical particles through a computation. The guidance exploits the high-dimensional structure of configuration space.

For Copenhagenists: the quantum computer performs operations on quantum states, and we measure the output. What happens in between is not our concern.

All three predict the same outputs. All three endorse the same algorithms.

The measurement problem is real and important. It does not stop us from building quantum computers. It stops us from fully understanding them.

IX. Current Research in Quantum Foundations

Quantum foundations is not a dead field. It is a growing one.

Bell inequality tests: Loophole-free Bell tests — Hensen et al. (2015), Giustina et al. (2015), Shalm et al. (2015) — have definitively ruled out local hidden variable theories. The universe is nonlocal. This is established.

Extended Wigner’s Friend experiments: Probing the consistency of quantum mechanics across multiple observers. These experiments are getting closer to testing interpretational differences.

Quantum Darwinism (Zurek): A framework for understanding how classical reality emerges from quantum mechanics through environmental selection. Not an interpretation but a framework for understanding why we see the world we see.

QBism (Fuchs, Mermin, Schack): Quantum Bayesianism — the wavefunction as a personal probability assignment, not a physical object. Increasingly sophisticated development of the “agent-centric” view of quantum mechanics.

Consistent Histories (Griffiths, Gell-Mann, Hartle): A framework for assigning probabilities to sequences of events (histories) without requiring a classical observer.

None of these has achieved consensus. All are active research programs.

X. Why This Should Matter to You

If you are a physicist: The measurement problem is an embarrassment we have hidden behind the success of the formalism. We teach students the rules without the reasons. We calculate without understanding.

This is pragmatically successful. It is intellectually incomplete. The measurement problem should bother you — not as a distraction from real physics, but as a sign that our deepest theory is telling us something we have not yet understood.

If you are not a physicist: The measurement problem is a window into something genuinely strange about reality.

The question “what happens when we look at something?” turns out to be one of the hardest questions in science. One hundred years of the best minds — Bohr, Heisenberg, von Neumann, Dirac, Feynman, Bell, Everett, Bohm, Zeh, Zurek — and we still do not agree.

This is not a failure. This is physics at its most honest: acknowledging that the deepest question about its most successful theory remains open.

Coda

The Schrödinger equation works. Perfectly. Every time.

We do not know why.

We do not know what the wavefunction is. We do not know if it is real or a calculational tool or an agent’s belief. We do not know if there is one world or many. We do not know if particles have positions between measurements. We do not know if the Schrödinger equation is exactly right or needs modification.

We know the predictions. We do not know the reality.

“Anyone who is not shocked by quantum mechanics has not understood it.”

Bohr said this. He was right. He also did not solve the problem.

Neither have we.

For the serious reader: Bell — Speakable and Unspeakable in Quantum Mechanics (1987) — the essential collection; Bell is the clearest writer on quantum foundations Everett — “Relative State Formulation of Quantum Mechanics” (1957) — the original Many-Worlds paper; remarkably readable Bohm — “A Suggested Interpretation of the Quantum Theory in Terms of Hidden Variables” (1952) — the original pilot wave paper Wallace — The Emergent Multiverse (2012) — the most rigorous defense of Many-Worlds Maudlin — Quantum Non-Locality and Relativity (1994) — the best philosophical treatment of Bell nonlocality Barrett — The Quantum Mechanics of Minds and Worlds (1999) — comprehensive survey of interpretations
entry.summary: The formalism works perfectly. Nobody agrees on what it means. A technical deep-dive into the most embarrassing open problem in physics.

entry.title: Nietzsche — God Is Dead, and We Have Killed Him

link.title: Nietzsche — God Is Dead, and We Have Killed Him

entry.content:
In 1882, a madman ran into a marketplace.

It was broad daylight. He was carrying a lantern.

He cried: “I seek God! I seek God!”

The people around him laughed. Has God got lost? Did he go on a voyage?

The madman turned on them:

“Where is God? I will tell you. We have killed him — you and I. All of us are his murderers. But how did we do this? How could we drink up the sea? Who gave us the sponge to wipe away the entire horizon? What were we doing when we unchained this earth from its sun? Whither is it moving now? Whither are we moving? Away from all suns? Are we not plunging continually? Backward, sideward, forward, in all directions? Is there still any up or down? Are we not straying as through an infinite nothing? Do we not feel the breath of empty space? Has it not become colder? Is not night continually closing in on us?”

He paused.

“I have come too early. This tremendous event is still on its way. It has not yet reached the ears of men.”

Friedrich Nietzsche wrote this in 1882. We are still catching up.

I. The Most Misread Philosopher

Nietzsche is the most misread philosopher in the Western tradition.

He has been claimed by Nazis — wrongly, by his sister Elisabeth, who falsified his letters and sold his work to the Third Reich. He has been claimed by self-help culture — wrongly, his aphorisms stripped of context and pasted onto motivational posters. He has been claimed by nihilists — wrongly, by readers who stopped at the death of God without reading what came after.

He has been used to justify domination, cruelty, nationalism, and the worship of strength.

He would have been contemptuous of all of it.

He wrote: “I am not a man, I am dynamite.”

He meant: his philosophy, properly understood, would explode every comfortable assumption — including the comfortable assumptions of those who thought they were his followers.

To read Nietzsche is to be made uncomfortable. If you are not uncomfortable, you are not reading him.

II. The Man

Friedrich Wilhelm Nietzsche was born in 1844 in Röcken, Prussia — the son of a Lutheran pastor who died of brain disease when Nietzsche was four years old.

He was a prodigy — appointed professor of classical philology at the University of Basel at twenty-four, before he had even completed his doctorate.

He was a close friend of Richard Wagner — a friendship that shaped his early work and whose disintegration shaped his mature work. When Wagner descended into German nationalism and Christian sentiment, Nietzsche broke with him completely — and wrote some of the most savage criticism of Wagner’s later work ever composed.

He suffered from chronic, debilitating illness — migraines, vision problems, digestive disorders — throughout his adult life. He wrote despite constant pain, often unable to read for more than an hour a day, dictating to assistants or writing in large script to spare his eyes.

In 1889, in Turin, he saw a horse being beaten. He threw his arms around the horse’s neck, collapsed, and never recovered his sanity.

He spent the last eleven years of his life in a state of mental collapse — first in an asylum, then in the care of his mother and sister.

He died in 1900, aged fifty-five.

The works that changed philosophy — Thus Spoke Zarathustra, Beyond Good and Evil, The Genealogy of Morals, Twilight of the Idols, The Antichrist, Ecce Homo — were all written between 1883 and 1888. Five years. In chronic pain. Nearly blind.

“What does not kill me makes me stronger.”

He meant it literally.

III. God Is Dead

“God is dead. God remains dead. And we have killed him.”

This is the most misunderstood statement in modern philosophy.

Nietzsche was not making an atheist argument. He was not saying: there is no God, therefore God is dead. He was saying something far more radical and far more frightening:

The belief in God — the Christian God, the moral framework that depended on God, the entire structure of values that had organized Western civilization for two thousand years — this belief has become unbelievable.

Not refuted. Not disproven. Unbelievable.

The scientific revolution, the Enlightenment, the historical criticism of the Bible — these had not exactly proved God false. They had made it impossible to believe in God in the way people had believed for centuries — with the naive, unreflective certainty that the cosmos had a moral structure, that human life had a divinely guaranteed purpose, that suffering had meaning, that death was not the end.

This is what Nietzsche means by the death of God.

And the consequences — which he saw with terrifying clarity — were not liberation. Not immediately.

The consequences were nihilism.

If God is dead — if the moral order that depended on God has collapsed — then on what foundation do we build values? What grounds morality? What gives life meaning? What prevents the conclusion that nothing matters?

The madman’s terror is real. He is not celebrating. He is mourning — and warning.

“The greatest recent event — that God is dead, that the belief in the Christian God has become unbelievable — is already beginning to cast its first shadows over Europe.”

The shadows are nihilism — the recognition that the old values have collapsed before new values have been found.

IV. The Will to Power

Nietzsche’s concept of the will to power (Wille zur Macht) is the most misunderstood concept in his philosophy.

It does not mean the desire to dominate others. It does not mean political power. It does not mean violence or conquest.

The will to power is Nietzsche’s account of the fundamental drive of all life — the drive toward self-overcoming, toward growth, toward the expansion of one’s capacities.

“What is good? — All that heightens the feeling of power, the will to power, power itself in man. What is bad? — All that proceeds from weakness.”

But power here means: the power to create, to overcome, to grow — not the power to destroy or dominate.

The highest expression of the will to power is the artist — the creator — who does not dominate others but overcomes themselves, who takes the raw material of experience and shapes it into something new.

The Übermensch — the Overman, the Superman — is not a master race. It is the human being who has overcome the herd morality, the resentment, the pettiness — who creates new values rather than inheriting old ones — who affirms life fully, without reservation.

Nietzsche’s examples of people approaching the Übermensch: Goethe. Napoleon. Caesar. Not because of their power over others — but because of their power over themselves, their ability to contain and transform suffering and contradiction into creative force.

V. Master and Slave Morality

In The Genealogy of Morals (1887), Nietzsche proposed one of the most provocative accounts of the origins of morality ever written.

He distinguished between two fundamentally different types of moral evaluation:

Master morality: The strong, the noble, the powerful define good as what they are — vital, active, creative, proud. They define bad as what they are not — weak, passive, reactive, base. The evaluation starts with the self. “I am good. You are bad.”

Slave morality: The weak, the oppressed, the resentful cannot overcome the strong directly. So they revalue values. They define good as what they are — humble, meek, suffering, self-denying. They define evil as what the strong are — powerful, proud, vital. The evaluation starts with the other. “You are evil. Therefore I am good.”

Slave morality is born from resentment — ressentiment — the festering of impotent hatred into a moral system that makes a virtue of powerlessness.

Nietzsche’s claim — provocative, controversial — is that Christianity is the great vehicle of slave morality.

The beatitudes: blessed are the meek, the poor in spirit, the suffering — these are, Nietzsche argues, the revenge of the weak on the strong — the transformation of powerlessness into a cosmic virtue.

“Christianity is Platonism for the people.”

This is not mere contempt. Nietzsche had enormous respect for the psychological sophistication of the Christian tradition — its mastery of the inner life, its understanding of suffering.

His argument is more precise: that the Christian moral framework, built on resentment and the denial of life, is incompatible with the full affirmation of existence that he believed was necessary.

VI. Eternal Recurrence

Nietzsche considered the thought of eternal recurrence his most important idea.

The thought experiment:

What if this life — this exact life, with all its suffering, all its joy, all its pettiness and all its greatness — had to be lived again, infinitely?

Not a similar life. This life. The same pain. The same mistakes. The same losses. The same moments of grace. Repeated, exactly, forever.

Could you affirm that?

Could you say: yes, again — not with resignation but with joy — not because everything was perfect but because you loved it enough to want it eternally?

This is not a cosmological claim. Nietzsche is not saying the universe actually recurs.

It is a test — the most demanding possible test of one’s relationship to life.

If you live in a way that, faced with eternal recurrence, you would say yes — then you are living well.

If you would recoil — if the thought of reliving your life fills you with horror — then you are not living as you should.

“My formula for greatness in a human being is amor fati: that one wants nothing to be different, not forward, not backward, not in all eternity. Not merely bear what is necessary, still less conceal it — but love it.”

Amor fati — love of fate.

Not acceptance. Not resignation. Love.

The full, unconditional affirmation of everything that has happened and will happen — not because it is all good but because it is all yours, and you would not be without it.

VII. Apollonian and Dionysian

In his first book — The Birth of Tragedy (1872), written at twenty-seven — Nietzsche introduced a distinction that has shaped aesthetics ever since.

The Apollonian: Order, reason, form, clarity, individuation. The principle of the beautiful illusion — the dream, the image, the individual. Named for Apollo — god of light, reason, prophecy.

The Dionysian: Chaos, ecstasy, dissolution, the primal unity. The shattering of the individual into collective, ecstatic experience. Named for Dionysus — god of wine, frenzy, transformation.

Great tragedy — Greek tragedy — holds both simultaneously. The Dionysian chaos is given Apollonian form. The Apollonian dream is penetrated by Dionysian truth.

Socrates, Nietzsche argued, killed tragedy — by insisting that reason could explain everything, that the Dionysian darkness was merely ignorance to be dissolved by argument.

The Socratic optimism — the belief that knowledge leads to virtue, that reason leads to happiness — was, for Nietzsche, a symptom of cultural decline: the replacement of tragic wisdom (the full acknowledgment of suffering and darkness) with shallow rationalism (the belief that everything can be fixed by thinking harder).

He later revised this view — acknowledged that his own early Romanticism had distorted it. But the distinction between the Apollonian and Dionysian remains one of the most generative ideas in the philosophy of art.

VIII. Perspectivism

One of Nietzsche’s most important epistemological contributions is perspectivism:

There is no view from nowhere. There is no God’s-eye perspective — no neutral, objective standpoint from which reality can be seen as it truly is.

Every claim to knowledge is made from a perspective — shaped by the knower’s drives, interests, history, embodiment, culture.

“There are no facts, only interpretations.”

This is not relativism in the simple sense — not the claim that all perspectives are equally valid. Nietzsche was fiercely evaluative: some perspectives are richer, more encompassing, more life-affirming than others.

But the claim to a perspective-independent truth — the claim that one’s own view is simply objective — is self-deception.

The honest thinker acknowledges their perspective, takes responsibility for it, and does not hide behind the fiction of objectivity.

This has been enormously influential — on philosophy of science, on literary theory, on feminist epistemology, on the sociology of knowledge.

The insight that knowledge is situated, that the knower is never absent from what is known — this is Nietzsche’s epistemological legacy.

IX. What He Got Wrong

Nietzsche was wrong about women — with a contemptuous confidence that is embarrassing to read.

He was wrong about many specific historical claims.

He was wrong, perhaps, about Christianity — his account is brilliant but one-sided, missing the genuine resources of compassion and community that Christian practice has provided.

He was wrong, almost certainly, about the Übermensch — the concept is too vague to be useful and too easily appropriated for purposes he would have despised.

And he may have been wrong about the relationship between his own philosophy and his madness — though this is impossible to know.

He wrote Ecce Homo — his autobiography — shortly before his collapse. Its chapter titles include: “Why I Am So Wise,” “Why I Am So Clever,” “Why I Write Such Good Books.”

It is either the most extraordinary act of self-affirmation in the history of philosophy — or the first sign of the breakdown. Possibly both.

X. What He Got Right

The death of God is real.

We live in a world that has lost the moral certainties that sustained previous centuries. The question of what grounds value, what gives life meaning, what we owe each other — these are genuinely open questions.

The naive religious answer is unavailable to most intellectually serious people. The naive rationalist answer — reason will provide — has not delivered.

Nietzsche diagnosed this situation with extraordinary precision. He did not solve it. But he named it correctly.

Resentment is a real moral danger.

The transformation of one’s weakness into virtue — the making of a religion out of one’s inability — is a genuine psychological and moral pathology.

The person who cannot create and therefore attacks creation. The person who cannot love life and therefore preaches its transcendence. The person who, unable to be strong, makes strength into evil.

These are recognizable types. Nietzsche’s analysis of resentment remains one of the most penetrating accounts of a certain kind of moral failure.

Amor fati is a genuine ideal.

The love of fate — the affirmation of one’s life in its entirety, not despite its suffering but through it — is one of the most demanding and most genuinely admirable ideals in the philosophical tradition.

Not the Stoic acceptance of what cannot be changed. Not the Christian hope for a better life elsewhere. The full, unconditional yes to this life, this suffering, this joy, this specific existence —

because it is yours, and you would not be without it.

Coda

“I know my fate. One day my name will be associated with the memory of something tremendous — a crisis without equal on earth, the most profound collision of conscience, a decision that was conjured up against everything that had been believed, demanded, hallowed so far. I am no man. I am dynamite.”

He was right that his name would be associated with something tremendous.

He could not have predicted what.

The uses of Nietzsche — the misuses, the distortions, the appropriations — are themselves a kind of testament to what he identified: the desperate human need for new values after the death of the old ones, and the danger of filling that void with the first strong voice that comes along.

He was dynamite. He did not control the explosion.

None of us does.

Further reading: Nietzsche — Thus Spoke Zarathustra (trans. Walter Kaufmann) Nietzsche — Beyond Good and Evil (trans. Walter Kaufmann) Nietzsche — The Genealogy of Morals (trans. Walter Kaufmann) — Kaufmann is the essential translator for English readers Walter Kaufmann — Nietzsche: Philosopher, Psychologist, Antichrist (1950) — the book that rescued Nietzsche from the Nazis Julian Young — Friedrich Nietzsche: A Philosophical Biography (2010)
entry.summary: On the most misread philosopher in history — will to power, eternal recurrence, and the Übermensch.

entry.title: Physics Lied to You — Common Misconceptions That Even Smart People Believe

link.title: Physics Lied to You — Common Misconceptions That Even Smart People Believe

entry.content:
Physics education has a dirty secret.

The way we teach physics — the metaphors we use, the simplifications we make, the intuitions we build in the first years — are often not just incomplete.

They are wrong.

Not wrong in the “technically this is a simplification” sense. Wrong in the “this will actively mislead you for the rest of your intellectual life” sense.

I am a physicist. I love physics. And I want to tell you about the things that physics education gets wrong — because correcting them is one of the most intellectually satisfying experiences available.

Prepare to unlearn.

I. Gravity Does Not Pull

Let’s start with the biggest one.

You learned: gravity is a force that pulls objects together. The Earth pulls you down. The Sun pulls the Earth. Massive objects attract each other.

This is Newton’s description of gravity. It is extraordinarily useful. It put men on the moon. It predicts the motion of planets with stunning accuracy.

It is also, fundamentally, wrong.

Einstein’s General Relativity — published in 1915 — replaced the concept of gravitational force with something far stranger and far more beautiful:

Gravity is the curvature of spacetime.

Massive objects do not pull on each other. They curve the fabric of spacetime around them, and other objects follow the straightest possible path through that curved spacetime.

When you fall off a building — you are not being pulled toward the Earth. You are following a geodesic — the straightest possible line through curved spacetime.

The floor is what is doing something unusual — it is accelerating upward, pushing you off your natural free-fall path.

This is not a metaphor. In General Relativity, the person in free fall is in an inertial frame — experiencing no force. The person standing on the ground is in a non-inertial frame — experiencing a force.

The floor pushes you up. Gravity does not pull you down.

Why does this matter?

Because it completely changes the conceptual structure. In Newtonian gravity, force acts at a distance — mysteriously, instantaneously, across empty space. In GR, there is no action at a distance. There is no force. There is geometry.

Spacetime curves. Things move along curves.

That’s it.

II. Space Is Not Empty

“The vacuum of space” — empty, cold, silent.

This is one of the most persistent misconceptions in popular science.

Space is not empty.

Even perfect vacuum — a region with no particles whatsoever — is filled with quantum fields.

Quantum field theory — the most precisely tested theory in the history of physics — describes reality not as particles moving through space but as excitations of fields that permeate all of spacetime.

The electromagnetic field. The electron field. The quark fields. The Higgs field. These fields are everywhere — including in vacuum.

And quantum fields in vacuum are not zero. They fluctuate.

Vacuum fluctuations — the spontaneous creation and annihilation of virtual particle pairs — are real, measurable phenomena.

The Casimir effect demonstrates this directly: two uncharged metal plates placed close together in vacuum experience an attractive force — because the vacuum fluctuations between the plates are restricted to certain wavelengths, while the fluctuations outside are unrestricted, creating a pressure differential.

This effect has been measured. It matches theoretical predictions. Empty space is pushing the plates together.

More startling:

The vacuum has energy — zero-point energy. And this vacuum energy appears to be what is driving the accelerating expansion of the universe — what cosmologists call dark energy.

The “emptiest” thing we know is churning with quantum activity.

Space is not empty. Space is a seething, fluctuating quantum foam that happens to have no particles in it.

III. Electrons Do Not Orbit the Nucleus

The image is iconic: a nucleus in the center, electrons orbiting around it like tiny planets. It is in every chemistry textbook. It is on the logos of nuclear agencies worldwide.

It is wrong.

Electrons do not orbit the nucleus.

In quantum mechanics, electrons do not have definite positions and trajectories. They are described by wave functions — probability distributions that tell you the likelihood of finding the electron in any given location if you measure it.

The electron is not going around the nucleus. The electron is in a quantum state — an orbital (note: not orbit, orbital) — that describes a probability distribution of where it might be found.

Some orbitals are spherically symmetric — the electron is equally likely to be found at any angle from the nucleus at a given distance.

Some orbitals are shaped like dumbbells. Some like four-leaf clovers. Some like donuts with a sphere through the middle.

None of them involve the electron moving in a circular path around the nucleus.

Why is the Bohr model — the planetary model — taught at all?

Because it gets the energy levels of hydrogen right. By accident. The quantization condition Bohr imposed — without theoretical justification — happened to give correct energy predictions for the simplest atom.

It fails for every other atom. And it gives a completely wrong picture of what an atom actually is.

The electron is not a tiny planet. It is a quantum field excitation in a superposition of positions described by a complex-valued wave function that collapses probabilistically upon measurement.

Much less paintable. Much more true.

IV. Lightning Absolutely Strikes Twice

“Lightning never strikes the same place twice.”

This is perhaps the most practically dangerous misconception in this list.

Lightning absolutely strikes the same place twice. Lightning preferentially strikes the same place twice.

Lightning is looking for the path of least resistance between the charged cloud and the ground. Tall, pointed, conductive objects — like trees, towers, skyscrapers — reduce the resistance for lightning discharge.

The Empire State Building is struck by lightning approximately 20–25 times per year.

The CN Tower in Toronto: approximately 75 times per year.

Lightning rods work precisely because lightning strikes the same place predictably — you give it a preferred path that directs the energy safely into the ground.

The saying presumably originated as encouragement — “don’t worry, you won’t get struck twice” — and somehow became treated as physics.

It is not physics. It is superstition with weather.

V. There Is No Sound in Space — But Not For the Reason You Think

“In space, no one can hear you scream.”

This is correct — but the standard explanation is incomplete.

The standard explanation: sound requires a medium — air, water, solid material — to propagate. Space is a vacuum. Therefore no sound.

This is true for the vast, sparse regions between stars.

But space is not uniformly empty.

The interstellar medium — the gas and dust between stars — is a medium. Very thin, but real. Sound can propagate through it.

In 2003, NASA astronomers detected pressure waves — sound waves — propagating through the hot gas surrounding the Perseus galaxy cluster.

The wavelength: approximately 30,000 light-years. The frequency: B-flat, 57 octaves below middle C — roughly \(10^{-15}\) Hz.

This sound wave has been propagating for approximately 2.5 billion years.

You cannot hear it — the frequency is far below the threshold of human hearing, and you would need to be floating in the thin hot plasma of a galaxy cluster.

But it exists.

Space is mostly silent. But “in space, no one can hear you scream” is a statement about the specific vacuum of interstellar space near Earth — not a universal law.

VI. We Do Not Have Five Senses

Aristotle identified five senses: sight, hearing, smell, taste, touch.

This is wrong.

Humans have many more than five senses.

Proprioception: the sense of your body’s position in space. Close your eyes. Touch your nose. How did you know where your hand was? Not sight. Proprioception — sensory receptors in your muscles, tendons, and joints that continuously report your body’s configuration.

Vestibular sense: the sense of balance and acceleration. Your inner ear contains the vestibular apparatus — three semicircular canals oriented in three planes — that detects rotational and linear acceleration.

Thermoception: the sense of temperature. Distinct from touch — you can feel heat without touching a warm object.

Nociception: the sense of pain. Distinct from touch — pain receptors respond to tissue damage, not contact.

Interoception: the sense of your internal bodily state — hunger, thirst, heart rate, the need to breathe. This is increasingly recognized as a distinct sensory modality, crucial to emotional processing.

Time perception: humans have a surprisingly accurate internal sense of duration, mediated by circadian rhythms and other neural mechanisms.

Some researchers count 21 or more distinct senses. The number depends on how you define “sense.”

Aristotle’s five is wrong. The exact number is genuinely contested. What is certain: we are far more richly sensory creatures than the ancient classification suggests.

VII. The Great Wall of China Is Not Visible From Space

This one is not physics — it’s geography. But it appears in so many science books that it deserves to be corrected.

The Great Wall of China is approximately 15–30 feet wide. From low Earth orbit — the International Space Station, approximately 400 km up — this is equivalent to trying to see a human hair from 3 kilometers away.

The wall is not visible from space with the naked eye.

This has been confirmed by Chinese astronaut Yang Liwei, who specifically looked for the wall during his 2003 mission and could not see it.

The wall is long — but length does not help visibility from orbit. Width determines visibility. Roads are also long and often wider — they are also not visible from space with the naked eye.

The misconception has been repeated so often that it appears in Chinese textbooks as a point of national pride. It is not true.

VIII. Mass and Weight Are Not the Same Thing

You have mass. You also have weight. These are different things.

Mass is a measure of the amount of matter in an object — more precisely, a measure of inertia (resistance to acceleration) and gravitational charge. Mass does not change with location. Your mass on Earth is the same as your mass on the Moon, the same as your mass in deep space.

Weight is a force — the gravitational force exerted on your mass by a nearby massive object.

\[W = mg\]

where \(g\) is the local gravitational acceleration.

On the Moon, \(g \approx 1.62 \text{ m/s}^2\) — about 1/6 of Earth’s. Your weight on the Moon is 1/6 of your weight on Earth. Your mass is unchanged.

In deep space, far from any massive object: your weight is approximately zero. Your mass is unchanged.

When astronauts on the ISS are “weightless” — they are not massless. They are in free fall — in continuous freefall around the Earth — and in a free-fall reference frame, the gravitational force is locally undetectable.

Their mass is entirely normal. Their weight — in the frame of the ISS — is zero.

This distinction is not pedantic. In general relativity, mass is a fundamental quantity. Weight is a frame-dependent quantity — it depends on your reference frame. Mass does not.

IX. Atoms Are Mostly Empty Space — But Not In The Way You Think

“Atoms are mostly empty space. If you scaled a hydrogen atom to the size of a football stadium, the nucleus would be a marble in the center.”

This is true and deeply misleading simultaneously.

The scaling is accurate. The nucleus is \(10^{-15}\) meters across. The atom is \(10^{-10}\) meters across. The ratio is \(10^5\) — one hundred thousand. An atom is mostly “empty” in the sense that the nucleus occupies a tiny fraction of the atom’s volume.

But “empty space” suggests that the rest of the atom is genuinely empty — vacuum.

It is not.

The electron is not a tiny particle orbiting at a distance from the nucleus. (We established this already — no orbits.)

The electron wave function fills the entire atom. The electron exists, probabilistically, throughout the volume of the atom.

When two atoms come close together — when you touch something — what prevents them from merging is not a physical collision between nuclei. It is the Pauli exclusion principle — the quantum mechanical rule that no two electrons can occupy the same quantum state.

The electrons of your hand and the electrons of the table cannot overlap states. This creates an effective repulsive force — the force you feel as “solidity.”

You are not touching the table. You are hovering, nanometers above it, repelled by quantum mechanics.

The table feels solid because of a quantum rule about the indistinguishability of electrons.

Physics is strange.

X. The Sun Is Not On Fire

The Sun is not burning.

Fire is a chemical reaction — rapid oxidation, the combination of a fuel with oxygen. The Sun contains almost no oxygen. There is nothing to combust.

The Sun is powered by nuclear fusion.

In the Sun’s core — under pressures of \(2.5 \times 10^{11}\) atmospheres and temperatures of \(1.5 \times 10^7\) Kelvin — hydrogen nuclei (protons) fuse together to form helium nuclei.

The mass of four protons is slightly greater than the mass of one helium nucleus. The missing mass — \(\Delta m\) — is converted to energy via \(E = mc^2\).

Every second, the Sun converts approximately 600 million tons of hydrogen to helium. In the process, approximately 4 million tons of mass are converted to energy.

\[E = (4 \times 10^9 \text{ kg}) \times (3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s})^2 = 3.6 \times 10^{26} \text{ J}\]

Every second.

This has been happening for approximately 4.6 billion years. It will continue for approximately 5 billion more years.

Fire, by comparison, releases approximately \(10^7\) joules per kilogram of fuel. Fusion releases approximately \(6.3 \times 10^{14}\) joules per kilogram — sixty million times more energy per unit mass.

The Sun is not on fire. The Sun is a thermonuclear reactor, 4.6 billion years old, 150 million kilometers away, converting mass directly to energy and sending a tiny fraction of it to warm your face on a spring afternoon.

That is better than fire.

XI. Evolution Does Not Have A Goal

This is biology, not physics — but too important to omit.

“Humans evolved from monkeys.”

More precisely: humans and other great apes share a common ancestor. We did not evolve from chimpanzees — we share a common ancestor with chimpanzees, from which both human and chimpanzee lineages diverged.

But the deeper misconception is this: evolution is not directed toward humans. Evolution does not have a goal. Humans are not the pinnacle of evolution.

Natural selection is a blind, undirected process. Organisms that reproduce more successfully pass on more genes. There is no direction. No destination. No progress in any meaningful sense.

Bacteria are not primitive forms on the way to becoming humans. Bacteria are extraordinarily successful organisms — more numerous, more metabolically diverse, and better adapted to more environments than any vertebrate.

If evolution has a direction — which it does not — it is toward bacteria, not away from them.

The tree of life has no trunk pointing toward us. We are one branch among millions — no more or less evolved than any other.

XII. The Speed of Light Is Not The Universal Speed Limit In The Way You Think

“Nothing can travel faster than light.”

This is approximately true — but needs precision.

The precise statement: No object with mass can be accelerated to or beyond the speed of light in vacuum.

This is because the energy required to accelerate a massive object approaches infinity as its velocity approaches \(c\).

But this constraint applies to objects moving through spacetime. It does not apply to the expansion of spacetime itself.

The universe is expanding. Distant galaxies are receding from us. Galaxies sufficiently far away are receding faster than the speed of light — not because they are moving through space faster than light, but because the space between us and them is expanding faster than light can cross it.

This is permitted. The speed-of-light limit applies to motion through space — not to the expansion of space itself.

There is also quantum entanglement — which produces correlations between measurements on separated particles that appear instantaneous.

These correlations do not transmit information faster than light — a crucial distinction. You cannot use entanglement to send a message. The correlations are real but cannot be controlled in a way that allows faster-than-light communication.

And phase velocity — the velocity of the phase of a wave — can exceed the speed of light. This does not transmit energy or information. It is a feature of wave mathematics, not a violation of relativity.

The speed of light is a deep and real constraint. It is also more subtle than the textbook version.

Coda: Why This Matters

You might think: these are technical corrections. Who cares whether electrons orbit or occupy orbitals? Whether gravity pulls or curves spacetime?

Here is why it matters:

The misconceptions are not just wrong. They point to a deeper pattern of thinking — the assumption that the world at its most fundamental must resemble the world at human scales.

That atoms are like tiny solar systems. That gravity is like magnetism. That the vacuum is like a room with the furniture removed.

But the world at quantum scales does not resemble the world at human scales. The world at cosmological scales does not resemble the world at human scales.

Reality is strange all the way down — and all the way up — and only by releasing the assumption that familiar intuitions must apply everywhere can we begin to understand what physics is actually saying.

The misconceptions are comfortable. The truth is vertiginous.

And the vertigo — the dizziness of genuinely confronting how strange reality is — is one of the most valuable experiences that physics can give you.

Welcome to the vertigo.

For further unlearning: Richard Feynman — The Feynman Lectures on Physics (free online) Carlo Rovelli — Seven Brief Lessons on Physics (2014) Brian Greene — The Fabric of the Cosmos (2004) Sean Carroll — Something Deeply Hidden (2019) For the mathematically adventurous: Misner, Thorne & Wheeler — Gravitation (1973) — the bible of General Relativity
entry.summary: Gravity doesn't pull. Space isn't empty. And lightning absolutely does strike twice. A physicist's guide to unlearning.

entry.title: Plato — The Cave, the Sun, and the Man Who Chose to Die

link.title: Plato — The Cave, the Sun, and the Man Who Chose to Die

entry.content:
There is a cave.

Inside the cave, prisoners sit chained — facing a wall. Behind them, a fire burns. Between the fire and the prisoners, people walk carrying objects — statues of animals, figures, things.

The shadows of these objects fall on the wall.

The prisoners have been here since birth. They have never seen the fire. They have never seen the objects. They have seen only the shadows.

For them, the shadows are reality. They have names for the shadows. They have expertise in predicting which shadow will appear next. They compete with each other in this expertise. They are proud of what they know.

Then one prisoner is freed.

He turns. He sees the fire — blinding, painful. He is dragged upward, out of the cave, into the sunlight.

He cannot see at first. Everything is too bright, too real, too much. Gradually, he adjusts. He sees the actual objects. He sees the actual world. He sees, finally, the sun itself — the source of all light, all visibility, all truth.

He goes back into the cave.

He tries to tell the others what he has seen.

They do not believe him. His eyes, readjusted to the dark, can no longer predict the shadows as well as they once could.

They think the journey has made him stupid.

If they could, they would kill him.

I. Plato and the Shape of Western Thought

Plato was born in Athens around 428 BCE.

Alfred North Whitehead, the mathematician and philosopher, wrote that the history of Western philosophy is “a series of footnotes to Plato.”

This is not hyperbole. It is barely metaphor.

Every major question Western philosophy has asked — What is real? What is knowledge? What is justice? What is beauty? What is the good life? How should we organize society? What is the relationship between the particular and the universal? — Plato asked first, and answered with a systematic completeness that has shaped the terms of debate ever since.

He did not merely ask questions. He invented the dialogue as a philosophical form — philosophy as conversation, as the encounter between minds, as the process of thinking together rather than the delivery of conclusions.

He founded the Academy — the first institution of higher learning in the Western world — which continued for over nine hundred years, until the Emperor Justinian closed it in 529 CE.

And he had a teacher whose death shaped everything he wrote.

II. Socrates — The Man Who Knew Nothing

Socrates wrote nothing.

Everything we know about him comes from others — primarily Plato, but also Xenophon and Aristophanes. The Socrates of Plato’s dialogues may or may not be the historical Socrates — scholars have debated this for centuries.

What is clear: Socrates was a man who walked the streets of Athens asking questions.

Not rhetorical questions. Not questions to which he had answers. Questions he genuinely did not know the answer to — or claimed not to.

He asked generals what courage was. He asked politicians what justice was. He asked poets what beauty was.

In every case, the person he asked began with confidence and ended in confusion — their assumed knowledge dissolved by the patient, relentless pressure of Socratic questioning.

This method — elenchus, refutation — was not designed to humiliate. It was designed to reveal: that what we think we know, we often do not know at all.

The Oracle at Delphi had declared Socrates the wisest man in Athens. Socrates investigated this claim by speaking to people assumed to be wise. His conclusion:

“I know that I know nothing — and in this, I am wiser than those who think they know but do not.”

This is the beginning of philosophy: not the possession of answers but the honest confrontation with ignorance.

The Athenians found this intolerable.

III. The Allegory of the Cave — A Close Reading

The Allegory of the Cave appears in Book VII of The Republic — Plato’s most ambitious work, a dialogue about justice that becomes a dialogue about everything.

The cave is not a metaphor for ignorance in the simple sense — for not knowing things. It is a metaphor for the structure of ordinary consciousness — for the way we all, inevitably, mistake appearance for reality, shadow for substance.

The prisoners are not stupid. They are us.

Their mistake is not a failure of intelligence — it is a failure of position. They are chained in a way that prevents them from seeing the sources of what they see. They see effects without causes, appearances without the things that generate appearances.

The shadows on the wall correspond, in Plato’s framework, to the world of ordinary experience — the world of particular, changing, impermanent things that we perceive through our senses.

The objects carried in front of the fire correspond to the mathematical and scientific objects — more real than the shadows, but still dependent on the fire (the intellect) that illuminates them.

The sunlit world outside the cave corresponds to the realm of the Forms — the perfect, eternal, unchanging archetypes of which everything in the sensory world is an imperfect copy.

The sun itself corresponds to the Form of the Good — the highest Form, the source of all being and all knowability, which Plato identifies, in some passages, with God.

This is the Theory of Forms — Plato’s most radical and most contested contribution to philosophy.

IV. The Theory of Forms

Consider a circle.

You have seen many circles — drawn on paper, traced in sand, formed by bent wire. None of them is a perfect circle. Every drawn circle has imperfections — slight irregularities, a width to the line, a material instantiation that falls short of the ideal.

But you know what a perfect circle is. You can recognize that drawn circles approximate it. You can measure how far they deviate from it.

Where is this perfect circle? Not in the physical world — it has no physical instantiation. Not in any mind — it is not a subjective impression.

For Plato: it exists in the realm of Forms — a non-physical, non-mental realm of perfect, eternal objects that the physical world imperfectly instantiates.

The Form of Circle is more real than any drawn circle — because it is what all drawn circles are trying to be, what they participate in, what makes them circles rather than squiggles.

Extend this to everything:

The Form of Beauty — of which beautiful things are imperfect copies. The Form of Justice — of which just acts are imperfect instantiations. The Form of the Good — the highest Form, which illuminates all other Forms the way the sun illuminates the physical world.

Mathematical objects — numbers, geometric figures — are the clearest examples of Forms. Seven is not a physical object. It is not in anyone’s head. It is necessarily what it is — eternally, changelessly. It is more real, in Plato’s sense, than any collection of seven apples.

The philosopher’s task — the freed prisoner’s task — is to turn away from the shadows, to follow the difficult path upward, to achieve knowledge of the Forms, and ultimately to contemplate the Good itself.

V. Why the Philosopher Must Return

The freed prisoner goes back.

This is the move that distinguishes Plato’s philosopher from the mystic or the pure contemplative.

Having seen the sun — having achieved the highest knowledge — the philosopher does not remain in the light. They return to the cave. They try to govern, to educate, to lead.

Not because the cave is pleasant — it is not, and they will be mocked and perhaps killed for their trouble.

But because justice requires it.

In The Republic, the philosopher-king — the person who has achieved knowledge of the Good — has an obligation to use that knowledge in the service of the community.

The highest knowledge is not private. It is political. It creates obligation.

This is one of the most contested claims in Plato.

Some readers find it authoritarian: who decides who the philosopher-kings are? Who guards the guardians?

Others find it the only coherent answer to the problem of political legitimacy: rule should be in the hands of those who know — not those who are wealthy, or popular, or born to the right family, but those who have genuinely understood the Good.

The tension is not resolved in The Republic. It is not resolved in political philosophy generally. It is one of the permanent problems of the relationship between knowledge and power.

VI. The Death of Socrates

In 399 BCE, Socrates was put on trial.

The charges: impiety (not believing in the gods of the city) and corrupting the youth of Athens.

The trial was political — Socrates had been associated with figures on the losing side of the recent civil conflict, including Critias, one of the brutal Thirty Tyrants.

The jury: 501 Athenian citizens. The vote: 280 to 221 for conviction.

He was given the opportunity to propose an alternative penalty.

He proposed that Athens should pay him a salary for his service to the city.

The jury, unamused, voted for death: 360 to 141.

He was sentenced to drink hemlock — a poison that causes progressive paralysis, working upward from the feet.

He had the opportunity to escape. His friends arranged it. He refused.

The Crito — one of Plato’s most powerful dialogues — records his reasons.

He had lived his entire life in Athens. He had accepted the benefits of Athenian law. To escape now would be to violate the implicit contract between a citizen and the laws of their city — to act as if the laws apply only when convenient, which would destroy the entire basis of law.

He would not do it.

He drank the hemlock.

The Phaedo records his last hours — a philosophical conversation with his friends about the immortality of the soul.

Socrates argues, with his characteristic combination of intellectual rigor and personal serenity, that the philosopher should not fear death — that the entire philosophical life is a preparation for death, a practice of separating the soul from the body, of prioritizing the eternal over the temporary.

His last words, to his friend Crito:

“Crito, we owe a cock to Asclepius. Pay it and do not neglect it.”

Asclepius was the god of healing. A cock was offered to him in gratitude for recovery from illness.

Socrates, dying, was expressing gratitude — for recovery from the illness of life, for the healing that death brings to the soul that has always sought truth.

Whether you find this beautiful or chilling depends on what you think about the soul.

What is undeniable: the man died as he had lived — without fear, without pretense, attending to the small obligations even at the last moment.

VII. What the Cave Gets Right

The Allegory of the Cave is not science. The Theory of Forms is not, in any straightforward sense, true — at least not in the way Plato meant it.

But the cave gets something permanently right.

We do not see reality directly.

We see appearances — filtered through our senses, our concepts, our culture, our language, our evolutionary history, our neurological architecture.

The history of science is the history of discovering that what seems obvious is not — that the sun does not go around the earth, that solid matter is mostly empty space, that time is not absolute, that the observer affects the observed.

The cave metaphor captures something that naive realism — the view that we simply see the world as it is — cannot account for.

Education is not the filling of an empty vessel.

Plato’s image of education — the turning of the whole person toward the light — is still the most profound account of what genuine learning requires.

Not the acquisition of information. The reorientation of attention, the development of new capacities for seeing, the willingness to be disoriented by what is more real than what you were accustomed to.

Knowledge creates obligation.

The philosopher who returns to the cave — this image of knowledge as the beginning of responsibility rather than its exemption — remains one of the most powerful arguments against the view that intellectual life is purely private.

If you see more clearly, you owe something to those who do not yet see.

What you owe, exactly, is the hardest question.

VIII. The Permanent Socratic Question

Socrates did not die for a doctrine. He did not die for a theory of Forms or a proof of the soul’s immortality.

He died for a practice — the practice of examining life, of refusing to accept unexamined assumptions, of following an argument wherever it leads regardless of the social cost.

“The unexamined life is not worth living.”

This sentence — from the Apology, Socrates’s speech at his trial — is the most provocative sentence in Western philosophy.

Not because it is obviously true. Because it is obviously debatable — and the debate it provokes is itself an instance of the examined life it commends.

Is the examined life better than the unexamined one? What counts as examination? Is the peasant who never reads philosophy but lives with integrity and love living an unexamined life?

Socrates would have enjoyed these questions. He would have had no final answers.

He would have kept asking.

That, for him, was the point.

IX. Plato’s Shadow on Everything

Every time you distinguish appearance from reality, you are doing something Platonic.

Every time you argue that there is a right answer — not just a popular one, not just a useful one, but a genuinely correct one — you are implicitly committed to something like the Forms.

Every time you believe that mathematics discovers truths rather than inventing them — that the Pythagorean theorem was true before anyone proved it — you are a Platonist in mathematics.

Every time you feel that justice is not merely what the powerful decide — that there is a standard against which even the powerful can be judged — you are reaching for something like the Form of Justice.

Plato is not a historical curiosity. He is the structure of certain ways of thinking that we have not yet found a way to abandon — because they capture something about how we actually engage with truth, with value, with the world.

Even those who reject him most forcefully — Aristotle, Nietzsche, the logical positivists — are defined by their rejection. You cannot escape Plato. You can only choose your relationship to him.

Start with the Apology — Socrates’s speech at his trial. Then the Phaedo — his last hours. Then the Republic — if you have the patience for it, it will repay you beyond almost any book I know. The Allegory of the Cave is Book VII — but read Books I through VI first. The cave means more when you know what came before it.
entry.summary: On the Allegory of the Cave, the Theory of Forms, and why Socrates drank the hemlock.

entry.title: Pushkin — The Man Who Invented Russian Literature

link.title: Pushkin — The Man Who Invented Russian Literature

entry.content:
Every Russian writer who came after him owes him a debt that cannot be repaid.

Dostoevsky said: “We all came from under Pushkin’s overcoat.”

Wait — that was Gogol’s overcoat.

But the sentiment applies equally to Pushkin. Perhaps more so.

Because Pushkin did not just write great literature. He created the language in which Russian literature would be written.

Before Pushkin, Russian literary language was a chaotic mixture — Church Slavonic, French borrowings, bureaucratic formulas, folk speech — with no agreed standard, no unified voice.

After Pushkin, there was Russian.

Not invented, exactly. Crystallized. The way a supersaturated solution, touched at one point, suddenly becomes crystal throughout.

Pushkin was the touch.

I. The Life

Alexander Sergeyevich Pushkin was born in Moscow on June 6, 1799.

His ancestry was complicated and he was proud of it: on his mother’s side, he descended from Abram Petrovich Gannibal — an African prince brought to Russia as a child, who became a general and favorite of Peter the Great.

Pushkin wrote about Gannibal. He was not embarrassed by his African ancestry. In an era of rigid Russian aristocratic identity, this itself was a kind of provocation.

He was educated at the Tsarskoye Selo Lyceum — a new institution, founded by Alexander I, intended to produce the new Russian intelligentsia.

He was a difficult student. He was also, by eighteen, already recognized as the greatest poet in Russia.

His first major poem — Ruslan and Lyudmila (1820) — was published when he was twenty-one. It was scandalous and celebrated simultaneously. Too irreverent. Too playful. Too brilliant.

Then came the exile.

His early poems — Ode to Liberty, The Village — contained political sentiments that the Tsar found unacceptable. He was exiled from St. Petersburg — first to the south (Kishinev, Odessa), then to his family estate Mikhailovskoye, in the northern countryside.

Six years of exile.

He wrote constantly.

The southern exile produced the Southern Poems — The Prisoner of the Caucasus, The Fountain of Bakhchisaray, The Gypsies — Romantic, Byronic, brilliant.

Mikhailovskoye produced the bulk of Eugene Onegin, Boris Godunov, and some of his greatest lyric poetry.

Exile did not silence him. It concentrated him.

He was eventually pardoned by Nicholas I — who became, in effect, his personal censor. Every major work required the Tsar’s approval.

This arrangement was humiliating. Pushkin accepted it because he had no choice.

He married Natalya Goncharova in 1831 — a woman of extraordinary beauty and, by most accounts, limited intellectual interests. Pushkin was besotted. The marriage produced four children and, eventually, a duel.

II. The Duel

On January 27, 1837, Pushkin fought a duel with Georges-Charles de Heeckeren d’Anthès — a French officer serving in the Russian imperial guard, adopted son of the Dutch ambassador.

D’Anthès had been pursuing Natalya Pushkina publicly for over a year. Anonymous letters — their authorship never definitively established — had mocked Pushkin as a cuckold.

Pushkin was consumed by jealousy and rage. He was also, by most accounts, exhausted — by debt, by the censorship, by society, by the grinding humiliation of his position.

He challenged d’Anthès.

He knew he would probably lose.

D’Anthès was a young, experienced military officer in his prime. Pushkin was thirty-seven, in poor health, not a duelist.

D’Anthès fired first. The bullet entered Pushkin’s abdomen.

Pushkin, wounded, steadied himself and fired. He hit d’Anthès in the arm — a glancing wound. D’Anthès survived.

Pushkin was carried home. He died two days later, on January 29, 1837.

He was thirty-seven years old.

On his deathbed, he said to his books: “Farewell, friends.”

Russia went into mourning. Nicholas I, fearing a public demonstration, had the funeral moved secretly, at night.

D’Anthès was expelled from Russia. He returned to France and lived to be ninety-three.

III. What He Did to the Russian Language

To understand Pushkin’s linguistic achievement, you need to understand the situation he inherited.

18th century Russian literature was written in a language almost no one spoke — a formal, Latinized, Church Slavonic-influenced idiom that was prestigious and dead.

At the same time, educated Russians spoke French among themselves. Russian was for servants.

There was a war — the “Shishkov vs. Karamzin” debate — between those who wanted to purify Russian by returning to Church Slavonic roots and those who wanted to modernize it along French lines.

Pushkin resolved this debate by ignoring it.

He wrote in a language that was simultaneously:

  • Rooted in living spoken Russian
  • Capable of the highest poetic expression
  • Free from artificial archaisms
  • Absorbing of foreign influence where natural
  • Flexible, precise, musical

He did not create a theory of language. He demonstrated a practice.

When Eugene Onegin appeared — when Russians read it — they recognized something:

This is how Russian sounds when it is fully itself.

The linguist Roman Jakobson wrote that Pushkin’s verse had achieved something virtually unique in literary history — the coincidence of the ideal poetic language with the living spoken language of its time.

This coincidence was not accidental. It was the achievement of a lifetime’s work.

IV. Eugene Onegin — The Novel in Verse

Eugene Onegin (1823-1831) is Pushkin’s masterwork.

It is a novel. It is a poem. It is written in a stanza form Pushkin invented specifically for it — the Onegin stanza: fourteen lines, iambic tetrameter, rhyme scheme AbAbCCddEffEgg (where uppercase = feminine rhyme, lowercase = masculine).

The Onegin stanza is to Russian verse what the Shakespearean sonnet is to English — a form so perfected for one specific purpose that it seems to have existed always, waiting to be discovered.

The plot, briefly:

Eugene Onegin is a young St. Petersburg dandy — bored, fashionable, disillusioned. He inherits an estate in the countryside. He befriends Vladimir Lensky — a young Romantic poet, idealistic, in love with Olga Larina.

Olga’s sister is Tatyana — bookish, dreaming, raised on French Romantic novels, utterly unlike the social world around her.

Tatyana falls in love with Onegin. She writes him a letter — one of the most famous letters in world literature — confessing her love with complete, devastating honesty.

Onegin rejects her. Kindly, but firmly. He is not capable of love. He tells her so.

At a party, bored, Onegin flirts with Olga to provoke Lensky. Lensky challenges him to a duel.

Onegin kills Lensky.

He leaves. Years pass.

When Onegin encounters Tatyana again — now a princess, married to a general, moving in the highest society — he falls desperately in love with her.

He writes to her.

She meets him. She tells him: she still loves him. She will always love him.

And she will not leave her husband.

“But I was given to another — and I shall be faithful to him forever.”

The novel ends.

V. Tatyana — The Most Important Woman in Russian Literature

Dostoevsky, in his famous Pushkin speech of 1880, called Tatyana the ideal of Russian womanhood — her sacrifice, her fidelity, her dignity.

He was right about Tatyana’s importance. He was reading her wrong.

Tatyana is not an ideal of self-sacrifice. She is the novel’s moral center because she is the only character who has genuinely developed.

At the novel’s beginning: she is a girl who has confused novels with reality, who has projected her romantic fantasies onto Onegin — a man she barely knows.

Her letter is beautiful. It is also naive. She is in love with an idea, not a person.

Onegin’s rejection is cruel. It is also, in retrospect, a gift.

When we meet Tatyana again, years later: she has become herself. Not the dreaming girl. A woman. Clear-eyed, dignified, without illusions.

She tells Onegin: “I love you — why should I dissemble? — But to another I was given; To him I shall be faithful forever.”

This is not resignation. It is integrity.

She knows what she felt. She knows what she feels. She also knows who she has chosen to be.

Pushkin’s sympathy is entirely with her.

Not because fidelity is a conventional virtue — Pushkin was not a conventional moralist — but because Tatyana’s refusal is an act of self-possession.

She refuses to be the object of Onegin’s belated desire. She refuses to repeat the mistake of her youth — of letting passion override judgment.

She is, in the end, more free than Onegin.

VI. Onegin — The Superfluous Man

Onegin gave Russian literature one of its central types: the superfluous man (лишний человек).

Intelligent, educated, capable of feeling — but incapable of sustained action, sustained love, sustained commitment.

He is bored by everything. He destroys what he touches — not maliciously, but through his inability to be present, to take anything seriously, to risk himself.

He kills Lensky not from malice but from social cowardice — he does not want to seem afraid to duel. He condemns his friend to death to preserve a social performance.

He rejects Tatyana — the one person who might have saved him from himself — because he is honest enough to know he cannot love her as she deserves.

Years later, when he finally feels — when Tatyana’s transformation awakens something real in him — it is too late. Not because Tatyana is cruel. Because Onegin cannot be trusted with what he claims to want.

Tatyana knows this. Onegin, in the final scene, may finally know it too.

The superfluous man — Pechorin in A Hero of Our Time, Oblomov in Goncharov’s novel, Rudin in Turgenev — all descend from Onegin.

Dostoevsky spent his career arguing with this type.

VII. The Lyrics

The novel and the long poems are what made Pushkin famous. The lyrics are what made him immortal.

Pushkin wrote several hundred lyric poems — love poems, political poems, meditations on death, nature poems, occasional verse, translations.

They are almost untranslatable.

The precision, the economy, the music — these are features of Russian that English cannot replicate.

But let me try to gesture at what they do.

“I loved you once…“ (1829):

Я вас любил: любовь ещё, быть может, В душе моей угасла не совсем; Но пусть она вас больше не тревожит; Я не хочу печалить вас ничем.

I loved you once: perhaps that love Has not entirely died within my soul; But let it not disturb you any longer; I have no wish to cause you any grief.

Eight lines. Iambic pentameter. The most famous love poem in the Russian language.

What it does:

It says goodbye without bitterness. It acknowledges feeling without demanding response. It releases the beloved without punishing her.

“May God grant you another love you so — As tenderly and truly as I loved you.”

The final lines are the most astonishing: the speaker wishes the woman he loves to be loved as well as he loved her — by someone else.

This is not resignation. Not martyrdom. It is the highest form of love — the one that wants the beloved’s happiness more than its own continuation.

In eight lines.

VIII. The Political Poet

Pushkin’s political poetry is less known in the West but central to his legacy in Russia.

Ode to Liberty (1817) — written at nineteen — attacked tyranny directly:

“Autocratic villain! I hate you, your throne, Your death, the death of your children — I see with cruel joy.”

This got him exiled.

The Village (1819) — on serfdom:

“Here torment-lordship drags along, Without feeling, without law, Appropriated by violent rod The labor, property, and time of the farmer.”

Also exiled.

But Pushkin’s political poetry is more complex than simple opposition.

He was not a revolutionary in the Decembrist sense. He was not a democrat. He believed in enlightened monarchy — a Tsar who governed by law and reason.

His political ideal was Peter the Great — the modernizing, Westernizing autocrat who dragged Russia into the modern world.

He wrote a poem in defense of Tsar Nicholas I after the Decembrist uprising.

This complexity — the combination of genuine hatred of tyranny with genuine monarchism — made him difficult to categorize then and makes him difficult to use politically now.

Both Soviet and post-Soviet Russia have claimed him. He belongs to neither.

He belongs to the language.

IX. The Translation Problem

Nabokov spent years translating Eugene Onegin into English.

The result — four volumes, including two volumes of commentary — is one of the most extraordinary works of literary scholarship ever produced.

It is also, by almost universal agreement, unreadable as a poem.

Nabokov made a decision: absolute fidelity to meaning over any attempt at verse form. He translated word by word, preserving every nuance of the Russian at the cost of all music.

He argued this was the only honest approach — that any attempt to replicate the verse would inevitably falsify the meaning.

Other translators — Johnston, Falen, Mitchell — have attempted verse translations. They are more readable. They are less accurate.

The lesson: Pushkin is the most Russian of writers precisely because his genius is most fully expressed in his language’s specific music.

To read Pushkin fully — to understand why Russians place him where they place him — you must, eventually, learn Russian.

This is both a challenge and an invitation.

X. Why He Still Matters

On June 6, Pushkin’s birthday, Russians gather at his statues across the country and recite his poetry.

Not organized events. Spontaneous gatherings. People who have memorized hundreds of his lines — because Russian schoolchildren memorize Pushkin the way English schoolchildren used to memorize Shakespeare — standing at his statue and speaking the lines aloud.

This has happened under the Tsars. Under the Soviets. After the Soviet Union.

It happens because Pushkin gave Russians something that cannot be taken from them:

A language that is fully itself. A literature that begins with him and passes through him. A way of speaking about love, death, freedom, and time that is Russian in the deepest sense — not nationalist, not ideological, but linguistic, musical, irreducible.

“And for a long time yet I shall be loved by people For having woken gentle feelings with my lyre, For having, in my cruel age, extolled freedom And called for mercy toward the fallen.”

He wrote this in 1836. One year before the duel.

He knew what he had made. He knew it would last.

He was right.

Start with the lyrics — any translation, just to hear the subjects. Then Eugene Onegin — Johnston’s verse translation is the most readable; Nabokov’s prose translation is the most accurate. Then The Bronze Horseman — the greatest short poem in the Russian language. Then, if you can, learn Russian. You will not regret it.
entry.summary: On the poet who created a language, wrote the first modern novel in verse, and died at thirty-seven in a duel he knew he would lose.

entry.title: Quantum Computing — Separating the Revolution from the Hype

link.title: Quantum Computing — Separating the Revolution from the Hype

entry.content:
I work on quantum error correction.

This means I spend my days thinking about how to build quantum computers that actually work — not in press releases, not in investor decks, not in breathless technology journalism — but in the real world, where qubits decohere, errors accumulate, and the gap between what we can do and what people claim we can do is measured in decades and orders of magnitude.

I love quantum computing. I believe it is genuinely revolutionary. And I am going to tell you why almost everything you have read about it is wrong, exaggerated, or misleading.

This is not pessimism. This is what honest quantum science looks like.

I. What Quantum Computers Actually Are

Let us start with what a quantum computer is — not the metaphor, the reality.

A quantum computer is a physical system that stores and processes information in quantum states.

Classical computers use bits — 0 or 1. Quantum computers use qubits — quantum states that can be in superpositions of 0 and 1.

A qubit state is:

\[|\psi\rangle = \alpha|0\rangle + \beta|1\rangle\]
where $$ \alpha ^2 + \beta ^2 = 1$$.

n qubits can be in a superposition of \(2^n\) states simultaneously. This is the source of the “exponential speedup” narrative.

But — and this is crucial — you cannot read out all \(2^n\) states.

When you measure the system, it collapses to a single state — with probabilities given by \(|\alpha|^2\) and \(|\beta|^2\).

The art of quantum algorithm design is not about storing \(2^n\) values simultaneously. It is about constructing interference patterns — using the quantum amplitudes — such that wrong answers cancel out and right answers reinforce.

This is extraordinarily difficult. Most problems do not have this structure.

Quantum computers are not “classical computers that try all answers at once.”

They are devices that exploit quantum interference to solve specific problems with specific mathematical structure more efficiently than classical algorithms.

II. What Quantum Computers Are Actually Good At

The honest list of problems where quantum computers offer provable or strong evidence of speedup:

Factoring large integers — Shor’s algorithm (1994).

Peter Shor showed that a quantum computer can factor an \(n\)-bit integer in polynomial time: roughly \(O(n^3)\). The best known classical algorithm (general number field sieve) runs in sub-exponential time — super-polynomial but sub-exponential.

For large enough integers, a sufficiently powerful quantum computer could factor numbers that are classically intractable.

RSA encryption depends on the difficulty of factoring. A large enough quantum computer could break RSA.

This is real. This is not hype.

It is also roughly 20–30 years away from practical relevance, as I will explain.

Searching unsorted databases — Grover’s algorithm (1996).

Grover’s algorithm searches an unsorted database of \(N\) items in \(O(\sqrt{N})\) time. Classical search requires \(O(N)\).

This is a quadratic speedup — real but modest. It does not break encryption directly. It halves the effective key length — which is addressed by simply doubling key length.

Simulating quantum systems.

This is the most immediately practical application and the one Feynman originally had in mind when he proposed quantum computers in 1982.

Simulating quantum chemistry — molecules, materials — requires classical computers to track exponentially many quantum amplitudes. A quantum computer could simulate quantum systems with polynomial overhead.

Applications: drug discovery, materials science, catalysis, battery technology, solar cells.

These are real, important, and closer than the cryptography applications.

Optimization problems.

Quantum annealing and gate-based quantum algorithms may offer speedups for certain optimization problems — logistics, finance, machine learning.

The evidence here is more mixed. For many optimization problems, the best classical algorithms are already very good, and demonstrated quantum advantage is limited.

III. The Hype Taxonomy

Let me categorize the quantum hype you will encounter in the wild.

Type 1: The Exponential Fallacy.

“A 300-qubit quantum computer can represent more states than there are atoms in the observable universe!”

True. Irrelevant.

Yes, 300 qubits span a Hilbert space of \(2^{300}\) dimensions. No, this does not mean a 300-qubit quantum computer is more powerful than all classical computers combined.

You cannot access \(2^{300}\) states. You get one sample from the output distribution per shot. The exponential state space is not your computational resource — quantum interference is.

And quantum interference only helps for problems with very specific structure.

Type 2: The Supremacy Confusion.

In 2019, Google announced “quantum supremacy” — their 53-qubit Sycamore processor performed a specific sampling task in 200 seconds that they claimed would take a classical supercomputer 10,000 years.

IBM disputed this, claiming the task could be done classically in 2.5 days with better algorithms.

Subsequent work has further reduced the classical simulation time.

The task Google chose — random circuit sampling — was specifically designed to be hard for classical computers and easy for quantum computers. It has no known practical application.

“Quantum supremacy” does not mean quantum computers are now better than classical computers at useful tasks. It means a quantum processor performed one specific, artificial, carefully chosen task faster.

This is a scientific milestone. It is not a revolution.

Type 3: The Timeline Compression.

“Quantum computers will break encryption in 5 years.”

No.

To break RSA-2048 with Shor’s algorithm, you need approximately 4000 logical qubits running reliable quantum circuits with millions of gates.

A logical qubit is a fault-tolerant qubit — protected from errors by quantum error correction.

Current state of the art: surface code error correction requires approximately 1000 physical qubits per logical qubit at current physical error rates (~0.1%).

So breaking RSA-2048 requires approximately: 4000 logical qubits × 1000 physical/logical = 4 million physical qubits.

Current state of the art: ~1000-2000 physical qubits.

We need three orders of magnitude improvement in qubit count, while simultaneously maintaining or improving error rates, coherence times, gate fidelities, and connectivity.

This is not 5 years. This is not 10 years. Serious researchers consider 20-30 years optimistic.

Type 4: The NISQ Oversell.

NISQ — Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum — refers to current quantum devices: 50-1000 qubits, without full error correction, with significant noise.

There is genuine research interest in whether NISQ devices can demonstrate quantum advantage for practical problems before full error correction is achieved.

This is a legitimate research question.

It has been aggressively oversold by companies (IBM, Google, IonQ, Rigetti) as imminent commercial quantum advantage.

The honest answer from the research community: NISQ advantage for practical problems has not been convincingly demonstrated.

The variational quantum algorithms (VQE, QAOA) proposed for NISQ devices have not outperformed classical algorithms on any problem of practical interest.

This may change. But it has not changed yet.

Type 5: The Investment Narrative.

Quantum computing has received billions of dollars in venture capital and government investment.

This creates strong incentives to:

  • Announce milestones aggressively
  • Describe research progress in maximum-impact language
  • Imply near-term commercial applications
  • Avoid discussing fundamental obstacles

I am not suggesting fraud. I am observing that financial incentives distort the public presentation of research progress in predictable ways.

Read quantum computing press releases with this in mind.

IV. The Real Obstacles

What actually stands between current devices and useful quantum computers?

1. Physical error rates.

Current best physical qubit error rates: approximately 0.1% per gate operation for the best superconducting qubits.

This sounds small. For a circuit requiring 1 million gate operations — typical for Shor’s algorithm at useful scales — the probability of at least one error is approximately:

\[1 - (1 - 0.001)^{10^6} \approx 1 - e^{-1000} \approx 1\]

Certainty of error. Without error correction, useless.

2. The error correction overhead.

Quantum error correction protects logical qubits from physical errors.

The most promising code for near-term devices: the surface code.

Surface code overhead at 0.1% physical error rate: approximately 1000 physical qubits per logical qubit.

This number improves as physical error rates decrease — but improving physical error rates is itself an enormous engineering challenge.

3. Decoherence.
